title_en: The Epistemology of Revelation: Certainty, Interpretation, and the Limits of Ta'wil
content_en:
The second major section of Dar Ta'arud addresses the epistemological framework through which both rational knowledge and revealed knowledge must be evaluated. Ibn Taymiyyah argues that the philosophers who prioritized reason over revelation were operating with a flawed understanding of what constitutes genuine rational certainty versus what is merely an assumption imported from Greek philosophy.

Ibn Taymiyyah distinguishes between different grades of rational knowledge. Some things are known with genuine certainty by reason — mathematical truths, logical laws, and basic perceptual facts. But much of what the Aristotelian tradition called 'rational certainties' were in fact contested philosophical positions, accepted by some schools of thought and rejected by others. When theologians such as ar-Razi treated Aristotelian metaphysics as equivalent in certainty to mathematical demonstration, and then measured Quranic texts against this supposed certainty, they were, Ibn Taymiyyah argues, smuggling a contestable philosophical tradition into a position it did not deserve.

He develops this argument through a careful analysis of the Aristotelian proofs that were most commonly invoked against Quranic texts. The proof for the negation of divine attributes, for example, rested on premises derived from Aristotle's physics and metaphysics — premises that were debated even within the Aristotelian tradition and that later philosophers (including Ibn Rushd) interpreted differently. To treat these premises as certain enough to override clear Quranic affirmations was, for Ibn Taymiyyah, a methodological error of the first order.

On the revelation side, Ibn Taymiyyah makes equally important distinctions. Not all transmitted texts carry the same epistemic weight. The Quran is mutawatir (transmitted by continuous, mass transmission) and its words are beyond doubt. The authentic hadiths (those meeting the criteria of sahih or hasan) are authoritative divine guidance. But the interpretation of these texts can vary, and legitimate scholarly differences in interpretation exist. The question of ta'wil (allegorical interpretation) requires judgment — not a blanket policy of either always accepting apparent meaning or always permitting reinterpretation.

Ibn Taymiyyah's position on ta'wil is nuanced and is carefully distinguished in this section. He does not deny that the Arabic language allows for figurative and metaphorical usage (majaz). He does not deny that scholars may differ on the interpretation of certain passages. What he objects to is the systematic application of ta'wil to all texts that appear to conflict with a predetermined philosophical framework, without considering whether the philosophical framework itself might be the problem.

He argues that the correct methodology is to examine both the rational argument and the revealed text together: if the rational argument is truly sound and the text is genuinely unclear, reconciliation through interpretation may be appropriate. But if the rational argument rests on contestable philosophical premises, and the text is clear and mutawatir, the rational argument should be re-examined — not the text. This is what he means by 'dar' (refuting) the claimed conflict: the conflict disappears once both sides are properly evaluated.

This section also introduces Ibn Taymiyyah's concept of al-'aql al-sahih (sound reason) versus al-'aql al-fasid (corrupted reason). Sound reason, properly trained on direct observation of reality and correct logical principles, will never genuinely contradict authentic revelation. The reason that conflicts with revelation is always a reason infected by mistaken premises, philosophical prejudices, or limited human comprehension of divine matters.
title_id: Epistemologi Wahyu: Kepastian, Interpretasi, dan Batas-Batas Ta'wil
content_id:
Bagian utama kedua Dar Ta'arud membahas kerangka epistemologis yang dengannya baik pengetahuan rasional maupun pengetahuan yang diwahyukan harus dievaluasi. Ibn Taymiyyah berargumen bahwa para filosof yang memprioritaskan akal atas wahyu beroperasi dengan pemahaman yang cacat tentang apa yang merupakan kepastian rasional yang tulus versus apa yang sekadar asumsi yang diimpor dari filsafat Yunani.

Ibn Taymiyyah membedakan antara berbagai tingkatan pengetahuan rasional. Beberapa hal diketahui dengan kepastian tulus oleh akal — kebenaran-kebenaran matematis, hukum-hukum logika, dan fakta-fakta persepsi dasar. Namun banyak dari apa yang tradisi Aristotelian sebut sebagai 'kepastian rasional' sebenarnya adalah posisi-posisi filosofis yang diperdebatkan, yang diterima oleh sebagian mazhab pemikiran dan ditolak oleh mazhab-mazhab lainnya. Ketika teolog-teolog seperti ar-Razi memperlakukan metafisika Aristotelian setara dalam kepastiannya dengan demonstrasi matematis, lalu mengukur teks-teks Qurani terhadap dugaan kepastian ini, mereka, menurut Ibn Taymiyyah, menyelundupkan tradisi filosofis yang dapat diperdebatkan ke posisi yang tidak layak diperolehnya.

Beliau mengembangkan argumen ini melalui analisis cermat atas dalil-dalil Aristotelian yang paling umum diterapkan terhadap teks-teks Qurani. Dalil untuk penafian sifat-sifat Ilahi, misalnya, bertumpu pada premis-premis yang berasal dari fisika dan metafisika Aristoteles — premis-premis yang diperdebatkan bahkan dalam tradisi Aristotelian sendiri dan yang para filosof kemudian (termasuk Ibn Rusyd) tafsirkan secara berbeda. Memperlakukan premis-premis ini sebagai cukup pasti untuk mengesampingkan penegasan-penegasan Qurani yang jelas adalah, bagi Ibn Taymiyyah, sebuah kesalahan metodologis tingkat pertama.

Pada sisi wahyu, Ibn Taymiyyah membuat pembedaan-pembedaan yang sama pentingnya. Tidak semua teks yang dinukil membawa bobot epistemik yang sama. Al-Quran adalah mutawatir (dinukil melalui periwayatan yang berkesinambungan dan massal) dan kata-katanya tidak diragukan. Hadis-hadis yang shahih (yang memenuhi kriteria shahih atau hasan) adalah petunjuk Ilahi yang otoritatif. Namun interpretasi teks-teks ini dapat bervariasi, dan perbedaan keilmuan yang sah dalam interpretasi itu ada. Pertanyaan tentang ta'wil (interpretasi alegoris) memerlukan penilaian — bukan kebijakan menyeluruh baik untuk selalu menerima makna zahir maupun untuk selalu mengizinkan penafsiran ulang.

Posisi Ibn Taymiyyah tentang ta'wil bernuansa dan dibedakan dengan cermat dalam bagian ini. Beliau tidak menyangkal bahwa bahasa Arab memperbolehkan penggunaan kiasan dan metaforis (majaz). Beliau tidak menyangkal bahwa para ulama boleh berbeda pendapat tentang interpretasi bagian-bagian tertentu. Yang beliau tentang adalah penerapan sistematis ta'wil terhadap semua teks yang tampaknya bertentangan dengan kerangka filosofis yang telah ditentukan sebelumnya, tanpa mempertimbangkan apakah kerangka filosofis itu sendiri mungkin yang bermasalah.

Beliau berargumen bahwa metodologi yang benar adalah mengkaji baik argumen rasional maupun teks yang diwahyukan bersama-sama: jika argumen rasional benar-benar sehat dan teks sungguh-sungguh tidak jelas, rekonsiliasi melalui interpretasi mungkin tepat. Namun jika argumen rasional bertumpu pada premis-premis filosofis yang dapat diperdebatkan, dan teks itu jelas dan mutawatir, argumen rasional yang harus dikaji ulang — bukan teksnya. Inilah yang beliau maksud dengan 'dar' (menolak) pertentangan yang diklaim: pertentangan itu hilang setelah kedua sisi dievaluasi dengan benar.

Bagian ini juga memperkenalkan konsep Ibn Taymiyyah tentang al-'aql as-shahih (akal yang sehat) versus al-'aql al-fasid (akal yang rusak). Akal yang sehat, yang terlatih dengan baik melalui observasi langsung terhadap realitas dan prinsip-prinsip logika yang benar, tidak akan pernah sungguh-sungguh bertentangan dengan wahyu yang shahih. Akal yang bertentangan dengan wahyu selalu merupakan akal yang terkontaminasi oleh premis-premis yang keliru, prasangka-prasangka filosofis, atau pemahaman manusia yang terbatas tentang perkara-perkara Ilahi.
content_ar: The ثانياً major الفصل of Dar Ta'arud addresses the epistemological framework through which both rational العلم and revealed العلم must be evaluated. ابن تيمية argues that the philosophers who prioritized العقل over الوحي were operating with a flawed الفهم of what constitutes genuine rational certainty versus what is merely an assumption imported from Greek philosophy. ابن تيمية distinguishes between different grades of rational العلم. Some things are known with genuine certainty by العقل — mathematical truths, logical laws, and الأساسي perceptual facts. But much of what the Aristotelian التراث called 'rational certainties' were in fact contested philosophical positions, accepted by some schools of thought and rejected by others. عندما theologians مثل ar-Razi treated Aristotelian metaphysics as equivalent in certainty to mathematical demonstration, and then measured القرآني texts against this supposed certainty, they were, ابن تيمية argues, smuggling a contestable philosophical التراث into a position it did not deserve. He develops this argument through a careful analysis of the Aristotelian proofs that were most commonly invoked against القرآني texts. The proof for the negation of divine attributes, على سبيل المثال, rested on premises derived from Aristotle's physics and metaphysics — premises that were debated even within the Aristotelian التراث and that later philosophers (بما في ذلك Ibn Rushd) interpreted differently. To treat these premises as certain enough to override clear القرآني affirmations was, for ابن تيمية, a methodological error of the أولاً order. On the الوحي side, ابن تيمية makes equally المهم distinctions. Not all transmitted texts carry the same epistemic weight. The القرآن الكريم is mutawatir (transmitted by continuous, mass transmission) and its words are beyond doubt. The authentic hadiths (those meeting the criteria of صحيح or حسن) are المعتمد divine الهداية. But the interpretation of these texts can vary, and legitimate العلمي differences in interpretation exist. The question of ta'wil (allegorical interpretation) requires judgment — not a blanket policy of either always accepting apparent meaning or always permitting reinterpretation. ابن تيمية's position on ta'wil is nuanced and is carefully distinguished في هذا الفصل. He does not deny that the Arabic language allows for figurative and metaphorical usage (majaz). He does not deny that العلماء may differ on the interpretation of certain passages. What he objects to is the systematic application of ta'wil to all texts that appear to conflict with a predetermined philosophical framework, without considering whether the philosophical framework itself might be the problem. He argues that the correct methodology is to examine both the rational argument and the revealed text together: إذا the rational argument is truly sound and the text is genuinely unclear, reconciliation through interpretation may be appropriate. But إذا the rational argument rests on contestable philosophical premises, and the text is clear and mutawatir, the rational argument should be re-examined — not the text. This is what he means by 'dar' (refuting) the claimed conflict: the conflict disappears حين both sides are properly evaluated. This الفصل أيضاً introduces ابن تيمية's concept of al-'aql al-صحيح (sound العقل) versus al-'aql al-fasid (corrupted العقل). Sound العقل, properly trained on direct observation of reality and correct logical principles, will never genuinely contradict authentic الوحي. The العقل that conflicts with الوحي is always a العقل infected by mistaken premises, philosophical prejudices, or limited human comprehension of divine matters.
